topic: World Religions: Judaism / Islam / Buddhism / Hinduism
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Is the Buddha worshipped as a creator god in Buddhism?
items.correct: No — the Buddha was a human teacher who attained enlightenment; Buddhists revere him as a guide rather than worshipping him as a creator god.
items.misconception: Buddhists worship the Buddha as the god who created the world.
items.random_wrong: The Buddha is the Hindu god of wisdom.
items.why: Students assume every religion centres on a creator god, but Buddhism is non-theistic at its core: the Buddha shows the path, he does not rule the universe.

items.q: Is Hinduism simply the worship of many unrelated gods?
items.correct: No — many Hindus understand the various deities as expressions of one supreme reality, Brahman, so Hinduism can be seen as one ultimate divine behind many forms.
items.misconception: Hinduism is pure polytheism with millions of separate, unconnected gods.
items.random_wrong: Hindus worship only one god called Krishna and reject all others.
items.why: Students count deities and conclude polytheism, missing the widespread Hindu view that deities are manifestations of the one Brahman.

items.correct: No — Muslims worship Allah alone; Muhammad is honoured as the final prophet and messenger, but he is human and is never worshipped.
items.misconception: Muslims worship Muhammad in the way Christians worship Jesus.
items.random_wrong: Muhammad is the Arabic name Muslims use for God.
items.why: Students map the Christian model of a divine founder onto Islam, but Tawhid forbids worship of anyone or anything besides Allah.

items.q: Which religion teaches the Four Noble Truths?
items.why: It's Buddhism. “Judaism” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: The first five books of the Hebrew Bible
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The first five books of the Hebrew Bible. “An Islamic law code” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Liberation from the cycle of rebirth
items.misconception: An annual pilgrimage to the Ganges
items.why: Correct answer: Liberation from the cycle of rebirth. “An annual pilgrimage to the Ganges” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these is one of the Five Pillars of Islam?
items.why: It's Sawm — fasting during Ramadan. “Baptism” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many Pillars of Islam are there?
items.why: Exact answer: 5 pillars. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
